title: Ranking Joe Biden’s mistakes
gist: Yglesias previews a podcast ranking the Biden administration's mistakes, taking the contrarian view that running for a second term and dropping out late was fine or even good, defending Biden's Israel-Palestine approach, and arguing the left's 'complicit in genocide' consensus is both factually wrong and a cope that lets progressives distance themselves without confronting the administration's real domestic-policy failures.

insights.claim: Yglesias takes the contrarian view that Biden's decision to run for a second term and then drop out too late to avoid an open primary was fine or even good, against the emerging Democratic-elite consensus.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Signals the post-mortem over what Democrats should change is genuinely contested, not settled - relevant to forecasting the party's 2028 direction.
insights.quote: the decision to run for a second term and then drop out too late to avoid an open primary was fine. It was maybe good, even.
insights.locator: I don't want to spoil the actual list

insights.claim: Yglesias defends Biden's Israel-Palestine approach and argues the left's rapidly-congealed view that Biden was complicit in genocide is both factually wrong and a cope that lets progressives distance themselves from his administration without grappling with its real domestic-policy failures.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Frames the genocide narrative as a deflection from substantive policy critique - a lens on how the left will or won't actually reform.
insights.quote: the rapidly congealed conventional wisdom on the left that he was complicit in a genocide is, I think, both factually wrong and also a kind of cope
insights.locator: I'm also a defender of Biden's approach

insights.claim: A superficial consensus among Democratic Party elites around being annoyed at Joe and Jill Biden is masking deeper disagreements about what the administration actually did wrong.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: The party hasn't truly diagnosed its failures, so 'we need to change' rhetoric lacks an agreed substantive direction - a source of continued strategic drift.
insights.quote: a superficial consensus around being annoyed at Joe and Jill is masking deeper disagreements about what, exactly, they did wrong
insights.locator: At the same time, my strong sense
